The SDE-Inverse Gaussian Model
  • Manuscript
  • Supplementary
  • Web app

A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability

The SDE-Inverse Gaussian Model

Authors
Affiliations

Matías Castillo-Aguilar

Escuela de Medicina, Universidad de Magallanes, Punta Arenas, Chile.

Noah Beelders

Department of Mathematics, University of Munich, Theresienstraße 39, 80333 Munich, Germany.

Cristian Núñez-Espinosa

Escuela de Medicina, Universidad de Magallanes, Punta Arenas, Chile.

Published

May 16, 2026

On this page

  • 1 Abstract
  • 2 Introduction
  • 3 Results
    • 3.1 The SDE-IG generative model
    • 3.2 Structural identifiability and parameter recovery
    • 3.3 Real-time autonomic state reconstruction
    • 3.4 Autonomic dynamics under a sustained stress protocol
  • 4 Discussion
  • 5 Methods
    • 5.1 Model formulation
    • 5.2 Parameter estimation
    • 5.3 UKF implementation
    • 5.4 Simulation study design
    • 5.5 Code availability

1 Abstract

Beat-to-beat cardiac variability encodes competing autonomic influences that existing models either summarize descriptively or track without explicit stochastic dynamics. Here we introduce the SDE-IG framework, which couples two Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes representing parasympathetic (\(a_p\)) and sympathetic (\(a_s\)) drives to an Inverse Gaussian first-passage observation model for inter-beat intervals. All eight free parameters are locally identifiable from a single RR-interval stream: the Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal and positive definite, a Monte Carlo recovery study (\(N = 250\) replications, 720 s recordings) confirms sub-percent relative bias and nominal 95% confidence-interval coverage, and a spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter reconstructs the net autonomic drive \(\hat\Delta(t)\) with a marginal-to-oracle RMSE inflation ratio below . Branch-level trajectories \([\hat p(t),\hat s(t)]\) emerge from the estimated spectral structure without additional data. The modular architecture extends naturally to multi-stream recordings. This framework provides an estimable, interpretable generative model for beat-to-beat autonomic dynamics.

2 Introduction

Heart rate variability (HRV) has long been used as a noninvasive window into autonomic cardiovascular regulation. Since the early quantitative work of the 1960s and 1970s, beat-to-beat fluctuations in cardiac period have been interpreted as signatures of vagal and sympathetic modulation acting on the sinoatrial node. Classical approaches emphasized time-domain statistics and spectral decomposition, linking high-frequency oscillations to respiratory vagal activity and lower-frequency components to baroreflex-mediated dynamics. While these approaches established HRV as a core physiological measure, they are fundamentally descriptive. They characterize second-order properties of a time series without specifying a generative mechanism for heartbeat timing.

A parallel line of research sought to embed cardiac rhythm in explicit stochastic models. Early integrate-and-fire descriptions of pacemaker activity motivated first-passage-time formulations in which each heartbeat is generated when a stochastic membrane potential reaches threshold. In that context, the inverse-Gaussian distribution arises naturally as the distribution of first-passage times for a drift-diffusion process. This observation provided a physiological bridge between cellular electrophysiology and statistical modeling of inter-beat intervals. Rather than treating RR intervals as realizations of a Gaussian time series, the heartbeat sequence can be viewed as a renewal process with a parametric inter-event distribution grounded in first-passage theory.

This renewal perspective matured with the development of point-process models for HRV. In influential work by Riccardo Barbieri and colleagues (Riccardo Barbieri et al. 2005), heartbeat times were modeled as a conditional point process with inverse-Gaussian inter-event intervals whose parameters evolved in time. That framework introduced a local likelihood formulation for nonstationary RR data and established a rigorous diagnostic toolset based on the time-rescaling theorem. By transforming event times through the integrated conditional intensity, model adequacy could be assessed via standard uniformity tests, providing a principled alternative to heuristic residual analysis. These contributions repositioned HRV modeling within the broader statistical theory of event processes and made explicit the likelihood structure underlying beat-to-beat variability.

Subsequent developments extended the point-process paradigm to accommodate covariates, history dependence, and adaptive filtering (R. Barbieri and Brown 2006). Time-varying parameterizations allowed heart rate to respond dynamically to respiration, blood pressure, or external perturbations, while maintaining the renewal structure of the observation model. These methods improved short-term prediction and enabled real-time tracking of instantaneous heart rate and variability. However, in many implementations, time variation was encoded through deterministic basis expansions or autoregressive updates of scalar parameters, rather than through an explicit stochastic dynamical system representing latent autonomic drives. As a result, although the models were statistically rigorous, their parameters were not always directly interpretable as physiological time constants or interaction gains.

Concurrently, state-space modeling became a central framework in systems physiology. Linear Gaussian state-space models and their nonlinear extensions were used to represent hidden regulatory processes underlying observable signals, including cardiovascular and respiratory dynamics. Stochastic differential equations, particularly the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process, became standard tools for modeling mean-reverting physiological fluctuations. The OU process offers analytical tractability and a clear interpretation in terms of decay rates and diffusion intensity, properties that align naturally with concepts of autonomic tone and variability. In many applications, however, state-space models were applied to uniformly sampled signals, and the irregular timing of heartbeats was addressed only after interpolation or resampling.

The conceptual integration of these strands, first-passage renewal models for event timing and continuous-time stochastic dynamics for latent regulation, remains incomplete in the HRV literature. Renewal models grounded in inverse-Gaussian first-passage theory provide a physiologically motivated observation distribution, but often treat time-varying parameters as externally specified or weakly structured. State-space models capture latent dynamics, but frequently rely on Gaussian observation assumptions that are not tailored to event data. The need for models that preserve the event-based likelihood while endowing time-varying parameters with explicit stochastic dynamics is evident in studies of nonstationary protocols such as exercise onset, orthostatic challenge, or recovery, where autonomic adjustments unfold over distinct and interacting time scales.

Another unresolved issue concerns interpretability and identifiability. As HRV modeling has grown more sophisticated, the gap between statistical flexibility and physiological meaning has widened. Highly adaptive models can fit complex RR patterns but risk overparameterization and ambiguous parameter interpretation. Conversely, overly restrictive models may fail to capture essential dynamics. The current state of the art therefore demands frameworks that are both generative and constrained: generative in the sense of specifying a complete probabilistic mechanism for heartbeat production, and constrained in the sense that parameters correspond to physiologically meaningful quantities that can be interrogated and, where possible, validated.

Within this landscape, the present work positions itself as an explicit synthesis of renewal-based point-process modeling and continuous-time stochastic state dynamics. It retains the inverse-Gaussian first-passage observation structure that anchors RR intervals in integrate-and-fire theory, while introducing coupled continuous-time latent processes to represent autonomic drives with distinct time constants and potential interactions. By formalizing these latent dynamics in continuous time and linking them directly to the event-level likelihood, the model aims to extend the methodological lineage initiated by renewal and point-process approaches, rather than to replace it. The central question is whether such a synthesis can provide additional interpretive power, through identifiable dynamical parameters, without sacrificing the statistical rigor and diagnostic tools that define the event-based HRV literature.

In doing so, this work contributes to an ongoing effort to move HRV modeling beyond descriptive variability metrics toward mechanistic, testable representations of autonomic control. Its position relative to the existing literature is therefore not that of a new summary index or purely predictive algorithm, but that of a structured generative model built directly on established theoretical foundations in first-passage processes, point-process statistics, and stochastic dynamical systems.

Here we show that this synthesis is achievable and productive. We introduce the SDE-IG framework and report four interlocking results. First, all eight free parameters are locally identifiable from a single RR stream, with a block-diagonal Fisher information matrix guaranteeing well-conditioned inference. Second, a Monte Carlo conditional recovery study (\(N = 250\) replications) confirms sub-percent relative bias and nominal 95% Wald confidence-interval coverage for all parameters. Third, a spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter reconstructs the net autonomic drive \(\hat\Delta(t)\) in real time, with a marginal-to-oracle RMSE inflation ratio quantifying the practical cost of latent-state integration. Fourth, forward simulation under a canonical stress protocol reproduces the asymmetric onset–recovery kinetics that are a hallmark of in vivo autonomic responses, with no model-specific adjustment beyond the structural difference \(a_p \gg a_s\).

3 Results

3.1 The SDE-IG generative model

The SDE-IG framework couples two Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) processes representing the parasympathetic (\(p(t)\), fast, HF) and sympathetic (\(s(t)\), slow, LF) autonomic drives to an Inverse Gaussian (IG) first-passage observation model for RR intervals. The autonomic state vector \(\mathbf{x}(t) = [p(t),\,s(t)]^\top\) evolves as

\[ \begin{aligned} dp(t) &= \bigl[-a_p\,p(t) - a_{ps}\,s(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_p\,dW_p(t) \\ ds(t) &= \bigl[-a_{sp}\,p(t) - a_s\,s(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_s\,dW_s(t) \end{aligned} \]

where \(W_p\) and \(W_s\) are independent Wiener processes. The net autonomic drive \(\Delta(t)=s(t)-p(t)\) enters the mean interval via the log link \(\mu(t)=\mu_0\exp(-\Delta(t))\), ensuring positivity without clipping and encoding the multiplicative structure of sinoatrial-node modulation. Given the previous beat at \(u_k\), the density of the next beat time is

\[ f(t \mid u_k,\mathbf{x}) = \left[\frac{\kappa}{2\pi(t-u_k)^3}\right]^{1/2} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{\kappa[(t-u_k)-\mu(t)]^2}{2\mu(t)^2(t-u_k)}\right\} \]

grounded in the first-passage-time interpretation of SA-node membrane dynamics (Chhikara and Folks 1976). The shape parameter is reparameterized as \(\kappa=\mu_0/\rho^2\), where \(\rho\) is the baseline coefficient of variation of intervals, giving eight estimable free parameters \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) (Extended Data Table 1; Methods).

The spectral density of \(\Delta(t)\) has two Lorentzian poles at \(a_p/(2\pi)\) Hz (HF, vagal) and \(a_s/(2\pi)\) Hz (LF, sympathetic). Cross-inhibition shifts both poles, generating a distinctive spectral fingerprint that distinguishes the coupled model from two independent OU processes (Supplementary Fig. S1). Because \(a_p \gg a_s\) at physiological rates, the two Lorentzians are well-separated and individually identifiable from a single RR stream.

3.2 Structural identifiability and parameter recovery

For the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps}=a_{sp}=0\)), the conditional log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks, parasympathetic OU, sympathetic OU, and IG observations, making the \(6\times6\) Fisher information matrix (FIM) block-diagonal by construction. Each \(2\times2\) block is positive definite analytically (Supplementary Note 1), confirming local identifiability of all six parameters from the state trajectory alone. Adding non-zero coupling (\(a_{ps}, a_{sp}>0\)) breaks block-diagonality; the \(8\times8\) FIM is computed numerically and remains positive definite at the reference parameter set (Supplementary Fig. S2).

We validated parameter recovery in a Monte Carlo study of \(N=250\) independent 720-second recordings generated under a canonical double-logistic stress protocol (Methods). The conditional MLE, applied given the true latent trajectory to isolate estimator precision from latent-state integration, shows sub-percent relative bias and 95% Wald confidence-interval coverage within 1–2 percentage points of nominal for all six parameters (Figure 2, Table 1). Profile likelihoods are strictly unimodal and sharply peaked for all parameters (Supplementary Fig. S3), confirming that the data contain sufficient curvature to locate the MLE precisely. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are similarly recoverable with nominal coverage in the full coupled model (\(N=250\); Supplementary Fig. S4).

Code 
source("R/filter.R")
FLT      <- pp_ukf(RES_UNCOUPLED$spikes, PARAMS_UNCOUPLED, CANONICAL_INPUT_FN)
flt_grid <- filter_to_grid(FLT, RES_UNCOUPLED$time)

layout(matrix(c(1,1,2,3,4), ncol = 1), heights = c(2.2, 1.2, 0.9, 0.9))
par(oma = c(3, 0, 0, 0))

# Panel (a): recovery dot-plot
par(mar = c(4.5, 6, 3, 5))
plot_recovery(REC_STUDY$summary,
              main = "(a)  Conditional MLE Recovery  (N = 250)")

# Panel (b): Delta recovery
par(mar = c(2, 5, 1.5, 2))
plot(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, RES_UNCOUPLED$delta, type = "l", col = "black", lwd = 1.2,
     ylab = expression(Delta(t)), xaxt = "n", frame.plot = FALSE,
     ylim = range(c(RES_UNCOUPLED$delta,
                    flt_grid$delta + 2*flt_grid$sd_delta,
                    flt_grid$delta - 2*flt_grid$sd_delta), na.rm = TRUE))
polygon(c(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, rev(RES_UNCOUPLED$time)),
        c(flt_grid$delta + 2*flt_grid$sd_delta,
          rev(flt_grid$delta - 2*flt_grid$sd_delta)),
        col = adjustcolor("#0072B2", 0.15), border = NA)
lines(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, flt_grid$delta, col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray60")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(expression("True"~Delta(t)),
                  expression("UKF"~hat(Delta)(t)~"\u00b1 2 SD")),
       col = c("black","#0072B2"), lwd = c(1.2, 2), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)
mtext("(b)", adj = 0, font = 2, cex = 0.9)

# Panel (c): branch reconstruction
sd_p <- approx(FLT$beat_times,
               sqrt(sapply(FLT$P_filt, function(P) P[1L,1L])),
               xout = RES_UNCOUPLED$time, rule = 2)$y
sd_s <- approx(FLT$beat_times,
               sqrt(sapply(FLT$P_filt, function(P) P[2L,2L])),
               xout = RES_UNCOUPLED$time, rule = 2)$y
ylim_b <- range(c(RES_UNCOUPLED$p, RES_UNCOUPLED$s,
                  flt_grid$p + 2*sd_p, flt_grid$p - 2*sd_p,
                  flt_grid$s + 2*sd_s, flt_grid$s - 2*sd_s), na.rm = TRUE)
plot(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, RES_UNCOUPLED$p, type = "l", col = "#000252", lwd = 1.2,
     ylab = "Branch states", xaxt = "n", frame.plot = FALSE, ylim = ylim_b)
polygon(c(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, rev(RES_UNCOUPLED$time)),
        c(flt_grid$p + 2*sd_p, rev(flt_grid$p - 2*sd_p)),
        col = adjustcolor("#0072B2", 0.15), border = NA)
lines(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, flt_grid$p, col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
lines(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, RES_UNCOUPLED$s, col = "#903900", lwd = 1.2)
polygon(c(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, rev(RES_UNCOUPLED$time)),
        c(flt_grid$s + 2*sd_s, rev(flt_grid$s - 2*sd_s)),
        col = adjustcolor("#D55E00", 0.15), border = NA)
lines(RES_UNCOUPLED$time, flt_grid$s, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray60")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("True p(t)", "True s(t)",
                  expression(hat(p)(t)~"\u00b1 2 SD"),
                  expression(hat(s)(t)~"\u00b1 2 SD")),
       col = c("#000252","#903900","#0072B2","#D55E00"),
       lwd = c(1.2,1.2,1,1), bty = "n", cex = 0.75)
mtext("(c)", adj = 0, font = 2, cex = 0.9)

# Panel (d): innovations
bt <- FLT$beat_times
plot(bt, FLT$innov, pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0,0,0,0.35),
     ylab = "Innovation (s)", xlab = "Time (s)", frame.plot = FALSE)
segments(bt, -2*sqrt(FLT$S_innov), bt, 2*sqrt(FLT$S_innov),
         col = adjustcolor("#D55E00", 0.4), lwd = 0.8)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray60")
mtext("(d)", adj = 0, font = 2, cex = 0.9)
mtext("Time (s)", side = 1, outer = TRUE, line = 1.5)

3.3 Real-time autonomic state reconstruction

The spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) processes one IBI at a time. The prediction step uses the exact Gaussian OU transition (no approximation); the update step applies sigma-point quadrature to the nonlinear observation function \(h(\mathbf{x})=\mu_0\exp(p-s)\) with IG measurement noise \(R_k=\hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\) (Methods). The marginal log-likelihood contribution is computed as \(\log\sum_i W_i^m f_\mathrm{IG}(\tau_k;\,h(\boldsymbol\sigma_i),\,\kappa)\) via log-sum-exp, avoiding both the linearization error of the Gaussian innovation approximation and the Jensen gap of the naïve lower bound.

The oracle filter (true parameters) achieves an RMSE of 0.1385 on \(\hat\Delta(t)\) (Figure 2; Supplementary Table S1). The posterior contracts tightest along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_-=[-1,1]^\top/\sqrt{2}\); per-branch variance along the common-mode direction is bounded by the OU stationary prior and remains finite, enabling recovery of individual branch trajectories \([\hat p(t),\hat s(t)]\) with per-branch posterior SDs reported in Fig. 2c.

The marginal MLE, estimating all parameters from spike times alone, uses data-driven spectral starting values and a reparameterized 5D optimizer that enforces \(a_p>a_s\) by construction and anchors \(\mu_0\) to the sample mean IBI (Methods). Across \(N=250\) replications the end-to-end pipeline achieves a marginal-to-oracle RMSE inflation ratio of 1.60× with a convergence rate of 100.0% (Supplementary Table S3), placing the pipeline in the well-identified regime where state estimation, not parameter uncertainty, is the dominant error source.

3.4 Autonomic dynamics under a sustained stress protocol

We simulated 720 s of autonomic activity under a double-logistic stress protocol (\(t_\mathrm{on}=300\) s, \(t_\mathrm{off}=420\) s, steepness \(k=0.1\) Hz) with the coupled full model (\(a_p=2.0\), \(a_s=0.2\), \(a_{ps}=0.15\), \(a_{sp}=0.10\) Hz; Methods). The onset of stress produces a marked rise in \(\Delta(t)\) driven by rapid vagal withdrawal (\(\tau_p=0.5\) s), followed by a slower sympathetic accumulation (\(\tau_s=5\) s) amplified by cross-inhibition. Cessation reverses this pattern asymmetrically: vagal restoration is rapid, while \(\Delta(t)\) returns to baseline gradually as sympathetic tone washes out (Figure 3). This hysteresis emerges entirely from the structural difference \(a_p \gg a_s\) and requires no model-specific adjustment.

A distinctive property of the IG observation model is heteroscedastic variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(\tau)=\mu(t)^3/\kappa\). As heart rate increases and \(\mu\) falls, beat-to-beat variability is suppressed cubically (Supplementary Fig. S5), providing a mechanistic account of the exercise-induced reduction in HRV without any post-hoc correction. Time-rescaling residuals confirm adequate model fit: the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic against Uniform(0,1) is 0.0354 (\(p=0.155\); Supplementary Fig. S6).

Code 
layout(matrix(1:4, 4, 1), heights = c(0.6, 0.9, 0.9, 1.4))
par(mar = c(1.2, 5.5, 1.2, 2), oma = c(3.5, 0, 2, 0))

plot(RES_COUPLED$time,
     sapply(RES_COUPLED$time, CANONICAL_INPUT_FN),
     type = "l", lwd = 2, ylab = "u(t)", xaxt = "n",
     frame.plot = FALSE, col = "black", ylim = c(-0.1, 1.3))
mtext("Autonomic Response to a Sustained Stress Protocol",
      outer = TRUE, cex = 1.05, font = 2)

plot(RES_COUPLED$time, RES_COUPLED$delta, type = "l", col = "#2C5F2E",
     lwd = 1.5, ylab = expression(Delta(t)==s(t)-p(t)), xaxt = "n",
     frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")

ylims <- range(c(RES_COUPLED$p, RES_COUPLED$s))
plot(RES_COUPLED$time, RES_COUPLED$s, type = "l", col = "#D55E00",
     lwd = 2, ylim = ylims, ylab = "Latent states", xaxt = "n",
     frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(RES_COUPLED$time, RES_COUPLED$p, col = "#0072B2", lwd = 1.5)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(expression("Sympathetic"~s(t)),
                  expression("Parasympathetic"~p(t))),
       col = c("#D55E00","#0072B2"), lwd = c(2,1.5), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

plot(beat_t, rr, type = "o", pch = 20, cex = 0.45, col = "darkgray",
     ylab = "R-R interval (s)", xlab = "Time (s)", frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(beat_t, mu_beats, col = "black", lwd = 2.5)
legend("bottomleft",
       legend = c("Observed R-R",
                  expression(mu(t)==mu[0]%.%exp(-Delta(t)))),
       col = c("darkgray","black"), pch = c(20,NA),
       lty = c(1,1), lwd = c(1,2.5), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)
mtext("Time (s)", side = 1, outer = TRUE, line = 2)

4 Discussion

The SDE-IG framework advances HRV modelling on three fronts relative to the existing point-process literature. First, time-varying parameters are driven by an explicit latent SDE rather than smoothed by a local likelihood window, so their dynamics carry a precise spectral interpretation: \(p(t)\) contributes power near \(a_p/(2\pi)\) Hz, \(s(t)\) near \(a_s/(2\pi)\) Hz, and the LF/HF power ratio equals \((\sigma_s^2/a_s)/(\sigma_p^2/a_p)\) to leading order, giving the widely used but often misinterpreted clinical index a direct generative meaning (Riccardo Barbieri et al. 2005). Second, all eight free parameters are recoverable from a single RR stream with sub-percent bias at physiological recording durations, a stronger identifiability guarantee than local-likelihood approaches that implicitly smooth over autonomic time constants. Third, per-branch posterior trajectories \([\hat p(t), \hat s(t)]\) emerge from the filter without additional observations, providing outputs unavailable from scalar point-process or spectral methods.

The framework connects naturally to the integrate-and-fire literature through the IG first-passage observation model (Chhikara and Folks 1976) and to the state-space HRV literature through the OU dynamics and Kalman-family inference. The accentuated-antagonism coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are identifiable conditionally on the state trajectory; their marginal identifiability from spike times alone, the harder question, requires a dedicated filter-based recovery study and is left for future work. The present results establish that the information is present in the state trajectory; the bottleneck, if any, lies in the latent-state integration step.

Several limitations bound the current analysis. The shape parameter \(\kappa\) is assumed constant across autonomic states; empirical data suggest mild state-dependence that would require a hierarchical extension. The exogenous input \(u(t)\) is known in simulation but unobserved in practice; the recovery study correctly sets \(c_p=c_s=0\) in both simulation and inference so that the pseudo-true and true parameters coincide, but the input sensitivities are not estimable in the single-stream protocol. Branch reconstruction is bounded by the OU stationary variance along the common mode; multi-stream recordings (respiratory, blood pressure) or longer recording durations reduce this residual uncertainty and enable fully separable branch inference. Real-data validation on PhysioNet benchmark recordings and quantitative comparison against the Barbieri 2005 model are underway and will be reported separately .

The modular architecture of the SDE-IG framework, OU state equation, log-link observation model, UKF inference engine, extends naturally to additional physiological streams. Each new observable registers as an additional likelihood term; the state equation is unchanged. In this setting the common-mode degeneracy that limits single-stream branch separation is broken by the additional measurement of an individual state (e.g., respiratory sinus arrhythmia amplitude for \(p(t)\), blood pressure for \(s(t)\)), fully separating the two branches and enabling estimation of the coupling terms from non-invasive recordings. This positions the SDE-IG framework as a principled, extensible foundation for the next generation of autonomic monitoring tools.

5 Methods

5.1 Model formulation

The SDE-IG model is defined by the coupled OU system above together with the IG first-passage observation model. The system matrix is \(A = \mathrm{diag}(-a_p,-a_s) + \bigl[ \begin{smallmatrix} 0 & -a_{ps} \\ -a_{sp} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\); linear stability requires \(\det(A) = a_pa_s - a_{ps} a_{sp} >0\), enforced throughout. Full derivations, including the log-link rationale, IG hazard computation in log-space, exact OU transition kernel, and survival-ratio event generation, are given in Supplementary Notes 1–4.

5.2 Parameter estimation

Given the latent trajectory, the log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks (Supplementary Note 1). OU parameters are estimated by one-dimensional profile likelihood over \(\log a\); IG parameters by closed-form score equations. Standard errors come from the analytical FIM.

Integrating over unobserved states, the total marginal log-likelihood \(\sum_k\log p(\tau_k\mid\Theta)\) is maximized by L-BFGS-B in reparameterized coordinates \((\log a_s,\; \log(a_p-a_s),\; \log A_p,\; \log A_s,\; \log\kappa)\), where \(A_\nu = \sigma_\nu^2/(2a_\nu)\) is the OU stationary variance of branch \(\nu\) and \(\mu_0=\bar\tau\exp(-(A_p+A_s)/2)\) is anchored to the sample mean IBI. This encoding enforces \(a_p>a_s\) by construction; \((A_p, A_s)\) are identified by the HF and LF spectral amplitudes respectively, near-orthogonalizing the five free coordinates (Supplementary Note 5). Data-driven starting values are obtained from biexponential ACF fitting and band-centroid spectral estimation; a two-stage wavelet Whittle pre-estimator is available for recordings with poor spectral resolution (Supplementary Methods).

5.3 UKF implementation

The filter uses the symmetric curvature rule (\(\alpha=1\), \(\beta=2\), \(\kappa=0\); \(2L+1=5\) sigma points for \(L=2\)). The prediction step is exact (linear OU); the update applies sigma-point quadrature with \(R_k=\hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\). The marginal log-likelihood is evaluated as \(\log\sum_{i>0} W_i^m f_\mathrm{IG}(\tau_k;\,h(\boldsymbol\sigma_i),\,\kappa)\) via log-sum-exp (Supplementary Note 6).

5.4 Simulation study design

All simulations used the canonical double-logistic protocol (\(t_\mathrm{on}=300\) s, \(t_\mathrm{off}=420\) s, \(k=0.1\) Hz, total 720 s). Input sensitivities \(c_p=-1.0\), \(c_s=0.1\) are treated as fixed known constants; the recovery study sets \(c_p=c_s=0\) in inference so that \(\theta_\mathrm{true}=\theta^*\) (Supplementary Note 7). The reference uncoupled parameters are \(a_p=2.0\), \(a_s=0.2\), \(\sigma_p=0.20\), \(\sigma_s=0.07\), \(\mu_0=1.0\), \(\rho=0.20\) (SI Table S1). The coupled model additionally sets \(a_{ps}=0.15\), \(a_{sp}=0.10\). Seeds for all \(N=250\) replications are drawn deterministically from master seed 2026.

5.5 Code availability

All R source code (model_functions.R, mle.R, filter.R, spectral_init.R, identifiability.R, sensitivity.R, validation.R) and the Quarto manuscript are available at .

References

Barbieri, R, and EN Brown. 2006. “SPECIAL ISSUE PAPERS-i Heart Rate Variability Signal Processing and Interpretation-Analysis of Heartbeat Dynamics by Point Process Adaptive Filtering.” IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering 53 (1): 4–12.
Barbieri, Riccardo, Eric C Matten, AbdulRasheed A Alabi, and Emery N Brown. 2005. “A Point-Process Model of Human Heartbeat Intervals: New Definitions of Heart Rate and Heart Rate Variability.” American Journal of Physiology-Heart and Circulatory Physiology 288 (1): H424–35.
Chhikara, Raj S, and J Leroy Folks. 1976. “Optimum Test Procedures for the Mean of First Passage Time Distribution in Brownian Motion with Positive Drift (Inverse Gaussian Distribution).” Technometrics 18 (2): 189–93.
